Common Issues With Matchmaking

This section discusses some of the common issues that title developers can encounter while working with Matchmaking. The discussion is presented in the following topics:

  • Title Issues
  • Attribute Issues
  • Query Issues
  • Filter Issues
  • Sort Issues

Title Issues

Session expiration setting too low

For Xbox-hosted games, sessions are automatically deleted when the host is restarted or turned off, so the session expiration setting should be left at the default of 86400. This setting is for PC-based servers where there is no automatic cleanup mechanism and is intended to prevent the existence of sessions for hosts that are gone but have neglected to delete their sessions.

Attribute Issues

Overuse of strings

Strings should not be used for attributes that are known at compile time and can be encoded as integers, because integers are much more efficient for the service. An example of a string that can be encoded as an integer is a game type, where 1 might represent Deathmatch, 2 Capture the Flag, and so on. An example of an attribute that is appropriate as a string is the gamertag of the host as the host name. String and binary block of data (BLOB) attributes cannot be used in query filters.

Query Issues

Finding friends

Matchmaking is generally not used to find friends, except in the case where the host address is looked up from a known session identifier. Using the XOnlineNotificationSetState function, each friend can set the session identifier of the session the friend is in and also set the XONLINE_FRIENDSTATE_FLAG_JOINABLE bit to indicate that the friend can be joined. This is the information that should be used to find a joinable friend. Once the session identifier of the friend is retrieved, you can use the XOnlineMatchSessionFindFromID function to find the session directly (to get the proper XNADDR structure and XNKEY key to connect to the friend). Or, if you need to return the attributes of the session to those found prior to connecting to it, you can create a custom XOnlineMatchSessionFindFromID query that searches for the session by session identifier.

Queries that look for a specific session

Queries that look for a specific session by session identifier should be of the XOnlineMatchSessionFindFromID type. Matchmaking normally only returns sessions with enough public available slots for the number of users logged on to the Xbox console running the query, and it also works under the assumption that it does not need to search the entire session space to find a desirable session. These assumptions are invalid in the case where you want to find a specific session, and creating the correct query type ensures that Matchmaking ignores these assumptions where appropriate.

Nearly similar queries

Rather than create several queries that are very similar, you can often create one query using null parameters as wildcards. Merging queries simplifies the client logic needed for Matchmaking and reduces the potential for errors. To do this, create your parameters with the option to allow null values, and in your client code pass X_ATTRIBUTE_DATATYPE_NULL as the parameter value when a wildcard is desired. You can often use one query for both Quick Match and OptiMatch using this method.

Quick Match returning too few sessions

Quick Match should return enough sessions to try several hosts in the case that the first one tried cannot be contacted (either because it is gone or there are issues with network address translator (NAT) traversal). An appropriate number is generally 10 or 15 sessions.

Filter Issues

Neglecting private slots in total player count

The number of current public slots in use should not be used as the total player count in a session. Keep in mind that if you are using private slots, the number of players in the session actually is the sum of public and private slots filled, and you may need to create a new attribute TotalPlayers, which you set with the sum of these two when you update the session, and then use that attribute in your filters.

Checking public slots

There is no need to check that the number of public available slots is above a certain number, because the Matchmaking server automatically does not return any session that does not have at least enough slots for the number of players logged on to a particular Xbox console. For example, if two players are logged on, Matchmaking does not return any session having less than two public slots available (unless the query was created as a XOnlineMatchSessionFindFromID query, in which case this check is ignored).

Sort Issues

Proximity sorting in general

If you have several sort operators, you should add a sort by rough network proximity at the top of your sort list and then sort by network proximity at the end of your sort list. When there are too many sorts above the network proximity sort, it diminishes the effect of that sort. However, adding a rough proximity sort to the top of the sort list can help make sure the network proximity sort has some effect on varying the results. It is important to make sure that every client searching simultaneously does not get back the same result list, especially when there are thousands of clients searching, because only some of those clients will get into a particular session and the majority will get back a message that the session is full. Network proximity helps to vary the results received by different clients searching at the same time and also helps clients find sessions close to them.

Sorts after a network proximity sort

Having any sort operator following the network proximity sort in the sort list generally means that the following sort operator is ignored, because network proximity is such a wide-ranging value and the effect of any sort operator decreases as the number of permutations above it increase.

Wrong sort direction on available public slots

If you sort by player count, it works best to sort descending by available public slots. Sorting the other way leads to bad results, because what happens in practice is that thousands of clients all simultaneously query and get the same set of sessions back, so that if there are only one or two open slots on the session, one or two of those clients get into the session and the rest get error messages saying the session is full. It is also better to sort by available public slots rather than current public slots in use, because the latter may not accurately reflect how many players are in the game as some may be occupying private slots.
